Title: Unique starch biosynthesis pathways in wild rice revealed by multi-omics analyses

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Australian populations of O. rufipogon; O. meridionalis
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Carbon import for starch biosynthesis in developing seeds
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Consistently upregulated in Australian wild rice versus Nipponbare, indicating altered precursor transport into plastids as part of distinct starch biosynthesis pathways
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Consistently higher in AWS during seed development
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pGlcT is one of the few genes consistently upregulated in both Australian wild rice species relative to Nipponbare. 2. It marks a core difference in precursor transport associated with distinct starch biosynthesis pathways in AWS.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although consistently differential, the paper does not directly connect pGlcT variation to specific digestibility phenotypes as strongly as GBSSI/BEI/SSIIIa
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential supporting target for precursor-supply engineering after validation

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Australian populations of O. rufipogon; O. meridionalis
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to amylopectin synthesis and starch physicochemical properties
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SSI expression differs among genotypes, especially higher in O. meridionalis at 25 DPA, but coding sequences are nearly identical, implying expression differences arise mainly from promoter/TF context
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Lowest in O. rufipogon; higher in O. meridionalis than Nipponbare at 25 DPA
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SSI expression varies among the three genotypes despite almost identical coding regions. 2. Promoter NAC and CCAAT motifs differ between AWS and Nipponbare. 3. Different expression of TFs such as OsNAC20, OsNAC26 and NF-YA8 may explain genotype-specific SSI expression.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes regulatory explanation rather than direct causal metabolite proof for SSI in slow digestibility
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential secondary target for starch quality tuning via promoter engineering

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from ADP-glucose to elongate linear alpha-1,4-glucan chains in amylose
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Determines eating/cooking quality and contributes to resistant starch and slower digestibility
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher expression and improved splicing efficiency in Australian wild rice increase amylose content and contribute to slower starch digestibility, soft gel consistency, high retrogradation and poor eating quality relative to Nipponbare
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in developing seeds of Australian wild rice than Nipponbare; most active in early seed development
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Lower in developing seeds of Nipponbare; most starch genes highest in early seed development
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Established major control gene for amylose content and eating/cooking quality
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GBSSI was one of the three essential starch genes more active in Australian wild rice than Nipponbare. 2. Australian wild rice carries a novel GBSSI haplotype (Wx-aw) distinct from eight Asian rice haplotypes. 3. Nipponbare shows intron 1 retention and a 3.3 kb pre-mRNA, whereas Australian wild rice lacks full intron 1 retention and shows greater isoform diversity. 4. Higher GBSSI expression and splicing efficiency explain the higher amylose content of wild rice and contribute to slower digestibility.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: High amylose and retrogradation are associated with poorer eating quality and harder texture after storage
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Wild rice GBSSI carries two functional SNPs not captured by the common Asian haplotype classification; AWS also has shorter CT repeats in 5'UTR associated with high amylose
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Use O. meridionalis/O. rufipogon GBSSI haplotypes or precise editing of splice/QTL variants to increase amylose and develop slowly digested rice, while balancing eating quality penalties
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq, Iso-seq isoform analysis, RNA-seq read coverage, haplotype analysis of 4726 accessions, comparative genomics, starch property association

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Australian populations of O. rufipogon; O. meridionalis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: ADP-glucose; short amylopectin A and B1 chains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Elongates short amylopectin chains to form longer B1 chains
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies structural determinants of starch physicochemical properties
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls starch cooking quality, especially gelatinization temperature
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Active ALKc allele in Australian wild rice increases SSIIa activity and contributes to higher gelatinization temperature and more intermediate amylopectin chains despite similar expression to Nipponbare
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: No major expression difference across genotypes reported; most active in developing seeds
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Similar expression across genotypes in developing seeds
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Major control gene for gelatinization temperature
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SSIIa expression did not differ significantly among genotypes, but AWS carries the active ALKc allele while Nipponbare carries the less active ALKa allele. 2. ALKc is inferred as the ancestral SSIIa allele. 3. Active SSIIa in AWS explains higher gelatinization temperature and contributes together with BEIIb to increased intermediate amylopectin chains.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: High gelatinization temperature may reduce preferred cooking quality in some markets
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ALKc predominates in many wild/indica backgrounds; domestication selected lower-activity alleles in japonica
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful allele for modifying gelatinization temperature and slowly digested starch when combined with favorable BEIIb/GBSSI backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative haplotype analysis, phylogenetic analysis, literature-supported functional SNP interpretation, starch property association

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza meridionalis; Oryza rufipogon; Oryza nivara
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Australian populations of O. rufipogon; O. meridionalis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Branched amylopectin chains, especially short transferred chains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces alpha-1,6 branch points by transferring short glucan chains, particularly DP6-7, during amylopectin synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Modulates gelatinization temperature and eating/cooking quality through amylopectin branch architecture
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher expression in Australian wild rice compared with Nipponbare contributes to more amylopectin branches and, with active SSIIa, higher intermediate B1 chains and altered starch quality
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Consistently upregulated in AWS vs Nipponbare during seed development
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Lower in Nipponbare during seed development
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for amylopectin branch architecture
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BEIIb is one of the three genes consistently upregulated in Australian wild rice compared with Nipponbare. 2. Asian rice has two major BEIIb haplotypes: BEIIbj and BEIIbi. 3. Phylogenetic analysis suggests BEIIbj originated from O. rufipogon and BEIIbi from O. nivara. 4. Higher BEIIb activity/expression together with ALKc likely promotes more intermediate amylopectin chains in wild rice.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered amylopectin structure can shift cooking quality away from preferred domesticated types
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: O. meridionalis BEIIb lies outside the Asian rice clade and has unique SNPs plus a 3 bp deletion
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Selection or editing of favorable BEIIb haplotypes combined with SSIIa and GBSSI can tailor starch quality and digestibility
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq, comparative genomics, haplotype analysis of 4726 accessions, phylogenetic analysis, starch structure interpretation

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Alpha-amylase family branching enzyme; CBM48-containing enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Amylose/amylopectin branching and chain distribution module
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Branched glucan chains; intermediate amylopectin chains; short amylose chains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces alpha-1,6 branches into glucans, contributing to short amylose chains and intermediate amylopectin chains
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly shapes fine structure linked to target digestibility
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Higher short-to-medium amylose and long amylopectin chain-related structures slow starch digestion
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher BEI expression in O. meridionalis contributes to more short amylose chains and altered amylopectin chain length, helping explain slower starch digestibility
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in O. meridionalis than Nipponbare and O. rufipogon, especially at 25 DPA
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as rate-limiting, but key determinant of fine-structure variation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BEI expression is specifically elevated in O. meridionalis relative to Nipponbare and O. rufipogon. 2. O. meridionalis contains a novel BEI haplotype with four unique functional SNPs. 3. Promoter indels generate stronger OsNAC24 motifs and different cis-element architecture, likely increasing BEI expression. 4. Elevated BEI is proposed to increase short amylose chains and contribute to slower starch digestibility.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher short-medium amylose chains may worsen eating quality and promote harder texture after retrogradation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CDS of Nipponbare and O. rufipogon are identical; O. meridionalis promoter rather than coding variation is emphasized as expression cause
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising target/haplotype from O. meridionalis for breeding slowly digested rice, especially via promoter editing to recreate strong CRE architecture without linkage drag
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq, promoter cis-element analysis, haplotype analysis, comparative genomics, literature-based functional interpretation

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Australian populations of O. rufipogon; O. meridionalis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Elongates branched glucan chains to produce longer amylopectin chains
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly shapes target product fine structure
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Long amylopectin chains contribute to slower starch digestion and starch quality differences
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher SSIIIa expression in Australian wild rice, especially O. meridionalis, contributes to increased long amylopectin chains and slower starch digestibility
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in AWS than Nipponbare at 25 DPA
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; key determinant of long amylopectin chain formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SSIIIa is more highly expressed in AWS than Nipponbare at late seed development. 2. SSIIIa shows high functional variant density and more haplotypes than SSI and SSIIa in Asian rice. 3. Higher expression in O. meridionalis is linked to high OsNAC24 and NF-YC12 expression. 4. The O. meridionalis SSIIIa haplotype is proposed as useful for breeding slowly digested starch cultivars.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher long-chain amylopectin may alter texture and cooking quality
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: O. meridionalis has far more exon variants than O. rufipogon relative to Nipponbare
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for introgression or promoter editing, ideally stacked with GBSSI and BEI to increase slowly digested starch
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq, haplotype network analysis, comparative genomics, promoter motif/expression association

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation via promoter binding motifs
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher OsNAC24 expression together with strong OsNAC24 motifs in O. meridionalis is associated with increased BEI and SSIIIa expression and slower starch digestibility
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-specific positive regulator contributing to O. meridionalis-specific slow-digesting starch architecture
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Improved carbohydrate quality via slower digestibility
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highest in O. meridionalis among the three genotypes
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Lower in Nipponbare and O. rufipogon than O. meridionalis
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native target promoters containing NAC motifs
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Promoter motif analysis, comparative expression analysis, prior reported DNA-binding evidence cited in paper
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. O. meridionalis has higher OsNAC24 expression than Nipponbare/O. rufipogon. 2. Strong OsNAC24 motifs are enriched in the O. meridionalis BEI promoter due to indels. 3. OsNAC24 is implicated in activating both BEI and SSIIIa, thereby contributing to slow starch digestibility traits.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential pleiotropic effects on other endosperm developmental programs are not assessed
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Inference combines motif presence, TF expression, and prior literature on direct binding rather than new binding assay in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for promoter engineering or coordinated regulatory tuning of BEI/SSIIIa to alter starch digestibility

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher OsbZIP58 expression in O. meridionalis is associated with elevated BEI expression and slower starch digestibility-related starch structure
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Supportive positive regulator of BEI rather than sole master regulator
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Improved carbohydrate quality via slower digestibility
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in O. meridionalis than Nipponbare and O. rufipogon
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative expression analysis, promoter motif interpretation, prior reported promoter binding cited in paper
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsbZIP58 expression is higher in O. meridionalis than Nipponbare/O. rufipogon. 2. OsbZIP58 is a reported BEI-binding factor and is proposed to contribute to elevated BEI expression in O. meridionalis. 3. This regulatory difference may help explain slower starch digestibility traits.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential broader effects on starch gene network not measured here
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Role in this paper is inferential from expression and known binding, not directly revalidated experimentally
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for regulatory manipulation together with OsNAC24 to elevate BEI expression

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Positive transcriptional regulation associated with CCAAT-box-dependent control
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher NF-YC12 expression in O. meridionalis is associated with elevated SSIIIa expression and increased long amylopectin chains linked to slower digestibility
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Supportive positive regulator of SSIIIa contributing to slow-digesting starch phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Improved carbohydrate quality via slower digestibility
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in O. meridionalis than Nipponbare; O. rufipogon differs at 25 DPA
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native SSIIIa promoter containing CCAAT-box motifs
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely NF-Y complex, specific partners not defined here
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative expression analysis and prior knockout evidence cited in paper
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NF-YC12 expression is elevated in O. meridionalis relative to Nipponbare. 2. Prior evidence cited shows SSIIIa is downregulated in nf-yc12 knockout. 3. The paper proposes NF-YC12 contributes to higher SSIIIa expression and long amylopectin chains in O. meridionalis.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unknown pleiotropy on endosperm development and other storage pathways
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct binding to SSIIIa promoter is inferred rather than directly demonstrated in this study
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulatory lever for increasing SSIIIa expression in quality breeding
